However, if you've noticed during the past week, we've opened up the main doors here for Isha. So, there's a couple of reasons for that, but we want to start using the main door when we come in, put our shoes there, put everything there, and then if someone needs to use the restrooms, they can cross across. for a couple of reasons, but one of those is so that the sisters as well can have their own entrance to enter from and they don't feel pressured so that they can put their shoes on the rack there and and have a you know their own entrance inshallah. But that's only going to work if we actually start using it. Now these things that Allah swears by they are not obviously they are not random. These things are not haphazard. These things are strategically placed in a chapter. They carry a particular tone, a very particular meaning and sometimes even a very particular rhythm that will match and escalate what is being said throughout the chapter. Here Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala has just concluded the verse speaking about the 19 angels that are the gatekeepers of the hellfire. Malik and his 18 subordinates and their subordinates below them. He spoke subhanahu wa ta'ala about the reasons that he mentioned these things and these details in the Quran. Now here Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala is giving us very specific imagery in our mind's eye. He's swearing by the moon. He's swearing by the night as it retreats. And I further swear by the dawn as it grows in intensity. So what is the imagery here? What are we collecting from this? I swear by the moon. And I swear the moon usually presents itself high in the sky and becomes prominent towards the middle or the second and final half of the night. And the night begins to retreat when the moon is in its fullest position in the sky. And then the night further retreats and recedes when the dawn starts to break and intensify. This is a parable for what? This is an example and a parable for the coming of the messenger, the arrival of the messenger. People were in jeileia. People were in a state of misunderstanding, of misguidance. People were in a state of worship of other than Allah, idolatry, superstition, indecency, bloodshed, misguidance, confusion. People were in a state where they disbelieved in the last day. And here Allah has appointed a messenger amongst them. And now the moon has grown in its intensity. the light of the moon, the night has no choice but to recede and retreat. And then the dawn breaks which is a parable for the sending of jirel with the verses of the Quran. The book of Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala is just the daybreak as it comes to do away with the night to do away with the confusion, do away with the guilt, do away with the hopelessness. People were suffering from incredible hopelessness before the Quran. And the Quran came back came to restore people and restore their hope in Allah and restore their hope in their actual potential. And so Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala swears by the moon. He swears by the receding and retreating night. He swears by the dawn as it grows in intensity. What is he says? Now, that imagery fits perfectly because people, they don't change. Human beings, we naturally fear change. It makes us feel we're not in control. We can't predict what's going to happen. And so, people are naturally adverse to change. However, this change, this change of the prophet Muhammad sallall.aii wasallam, this change of jahyah, this change of the Quran, it's a big change and it's painful at times. However, it is the best of change. It is necessary change. It is just as necessary and just as beautiful and just as grand and illustrious as the morning sun breaking the dawn, as the night retreating with its coldness and with its stillness and with its lifelessness and the moon being outshined by the sun. We got used to things in night. We got used to things and we started merely surviving. But the Quran came to teach us how to live. Not just to exist, not just to survive, but how to live with hearts filled with hope and reliance upon Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. And so this change may be fought against, but it is change for the better. Second of all, this change is inevitable. It is just as inevitable as the sun rising. It is not something you can stop, oh people of Quraysh, those who have chosen amongst you to fight the message and fight against the messenger and try to hinder the path of Allah. Your fight is as fruitless as it is in trying to stop the sun from rising at morning. It's impossible. It is inevitable. It is not just a force of nature. It is beyond that. It is the will of almighty Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala. And so if you choose to embrace it, you will lust bluster in the sun. And if you choose to fight against it, your fight is just as hopeless as the night's fight against the morning sun. Alhamdulillah.

summary: This video contains announcements from the Muslim Community Center of Tucson followed by a detailed Quranic exegesis (tafsir) of Surah Al-Muddaththir, verses 32-37. The announcements include plans for collective Quran reading sessions (Juz by Juz) and facility updates regarding entrance usage. The main content focuses on the interpretive explanation of Allah's oaths in the passage, specifically the imagery of the moon, retreating night, and breaking dawn, which serve as a parable for the arrival of Prophet Muhammad and the revelation of the Quran as a transformative force that brings people from darkness and misguidance into light and hope.
suggested_title: Tafsir of Surah Al-Muddaththir (verses 32-37): The Imagery of Divine Oaths and the Coming of the Messenger
catchy_title: Moon, Night, Dawn: Understanding Divine Oaths
